title: [ipv6_addr.c:136-155] ULA prefix validation only checks first byte, not /8 boundary
description:
Category not checked: IPv6 address handling edge cases

Bug found: lichen_make_ula() validates prefix[0] == 0xfd (line 152), but the comment says 'fd00::/8' which implies ALL 8 bits of the first byte. Actually fd00::/8 means:
- First byte must be 0xfd (correct check)

But wait - /8 means first 8 bits, which IS the first byte. So prefix[0] == 0xfd is correct.

However, the function copies all 8 bytes of prefix without validating bytes 1-7. RFC 4193 says:
- Bits 0-7: 0xfd (L=1)
- Bits 8-15: Global ID high byte
- Bits 16-55: Global ID (40 bits total)
- Bits 56-63: Subnet ID

The function doesn't validate that bytes 1-7 form a valid Global ID. An all-zeros Global ID (fd00:0000:0000::) is technically valid but collides with other implementations using the same. A proper ULA should use a pseudo-random Global ID per RFC 4193 Section 3.2.2.

Impact: P4 - Allows potentially colliding ULA prefixes, but this is arguably intentional simplicity.
close_reason: Fixed: Added comment documenting that Global ID validation is intentionally omitted - deployment policy issue, not protocol violation
